mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive overview of the social sciences as a cluster of disciplines tracing from 18th-century origins (A.1) through 19th-century disciplinary formation (A.2) to 20th-century methodological developments including quantitative methods and computational tools (A.3.c, A.3.d.iii, A.3.d.iv). Covers positivist and interpretivist approaches and the role of social science in policy and governance (A.3.d.ii).

Directly addresses the nature and development of sociology (C) from Comte, Marx, Spencer, and Durkheim (C.1), through functionalism, conflict theory, and structuralism (A.3.e, C.2), to contemporary subfields including criminology and social psychology (C.5). The discipline's 19th-century emergence as a separate discipline (A.2.b) and its 20th-century specialization and cross-disciplinary approaches (A.3.d) are covered.

Directly addresses the nature of political science (E), its emergence as an academic discipline in the late 19th century (E.1), and its contemporary scope and methods including quantitative approaches, formal modelling, and surveys (E.2, A.3.d.iii, A.3.d.iv). Covers cognate disciplines including international relations, public administration, and public policy (E.3).

Directly covers the nature of economics (D), including the history of economic thought from classical through Keynesian and post-2008 frameworks (D.1), the scope of microeconomics and macroeconomics (D.2), and cognate disciplines including behavioral and mathematical economics (D.3). Its 19th-century development as a separate discipline aligns with A.2.b.

Directly addresses the nature of anthropology (B), its background (B.1), and the division between cultural and physical anthropology with ethnographic methods (B.2). The discipline's 19th-century emergence as a separate discipline aligns with A.2.b.

Covers legislative, executive, and judicial branches, constitutional frameworks, public administration, and forms of governance including democracy, monarchy, and autocracy, relevant to the cognate disciplines of political science including political systems and public administration (E.3).
Migration intersects with demography (C.5) as a cognate discipline of sociology, and draws on theoretical frameworks from labor economics and political science that cross social science boundaries.

Historical materialism and class struggle theory represent a foundational 19th-century influence on social, political, and economic theories (A.2.a). Marxism's continuing 20th-century influence on the social sciences is directly addressed (A.3.b). Marx shaped the background of sociology (C.1) and the development of economic theories (D.1).

The survey of systematic rational inquiry from Presocratic thought through Enlightenment traditions provides intellectual context for the origins of the social sciences in Renaissance and Enlightenment thought (A.1.a, A.1.b).

Marxist class analysis (bourgeoisie vs. proletariat) and Weberian stratification theory represent key 19th-century social theories (A.2.a) and the continuing influence of Marxism (A.3.b). Class stratification is a core subject of sociological methodology and theory (C.2).

The full range of human behavioral responses across psychological, social, and cultural domains maps to the nature and scope of psychology (G.2) and intersects with social psychology as a cognate discipline of both psychology and sociology (G.3, C.5).

Directly addresses the nature of geography (F), its historical roots in Greek scholarship (F.1), and the division between physical, human, and regional geography with spatial analysis traditions (F.2).

Social statistics developed as a separate discipline in the 19th century (A.2.b), and the introduction of mathematical and quantitative methods including statistical computing became central to 20th-century social sciences (A.3.d.iii).
Emotion as a subject studied across psychology and neuroscience falls within the nature and scope of psychology (G.2), with connections to social psychology as a cognate discipline (G.3).

The state as a political entity exercising sovereign authority, with theories ranging from social contract to Marxist and pluralist models, is a core subject of contemporary political science (E.2) and its cognate disciplines including political systems and international relations (E.3).

Kant's Enlightenment philosophy of reason, political philosophy including perpetual peace, and epistemological contributions represent the heritage of the Enlightenment that shaped social reforms and the intellectual preconditions for social science (A.1.b).

The Enlightenment's emphasis on reason, empirical inquiry, and rational reform of social and political institutions directly constitutes the heritage of the Enlightenment that gave rise to the social sciences (A.1.b).

The shift to information-technology-centred economies, computing, and data-driven analysis connects to the introduction of mathematical methods and the use of computers in 20th-century social sciences (A.3.d.iii).

Gender as a social construct examined through feminist theory, developmental psychology, and sociology connects to cultural anthropology's scope and methods (B.2) and the methodology of contemporary sociology (C.2).

The concept of society as a persistent group with social relations, division of labor, and theoretical frameworks including functionalism and conflict theory maps directly to the nature, background, and methodology of sociology (C, C.1, C.2).
Public law, constitutional law, and the intersection of law with sociology and political institutions connect to the cognate disciplines of political science including public law (E.3).
Machiavelli's empirical analysis of power in the Renaissance represents a precursor to the social sciences (A.1.a) and a foundational figure in the history of political science (E.1).
Locke's social contract theory, empiricism, and natural rights philosophy represent core Enlightenment contributions (A.1.b) that shaped the history of political science (E.1).

Freud's psychoanalytic theory and its influence on understanding the unconscious, repression, and cultural analysis directly constitutes the influence of Freudian ideas on 20th-century social sciences (A.3.c) and the history of psychology (G.1).

The extension of power over foreign peoples, anti-imperialist movements, and theories of economic imperialism (Lenin, Hobson) connect to the influence of social upheaval in the non-Western world and the revolution of rising expectations (A.3.a).

Culture as the symbolic and normative content of collective life, studied through anthropology and sociology, connects to the scope and methods of anthropology (B.2) and the methodology of sociology (C.2).
Ethnicity as socioculturally constructed identification studied through primordialism and constructivism connects to cultural anthropology's scope (B.2) and sociological methodology (C.2).

The activities and systems of collective decision-making and power distribution, covering political theory, forms of government, and the left-right spectrum, map directly to the nature and scope of contemporary political science (E, E.2).

Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah introduced systematic theories of historical causation, group solidarity (asabiyyah), and proto-macroeconomics, representing a precursor of the social sciences in the Middle Ages (A.1.a) and a forerunner of sociology (C.1).

Smith's Wealth of Nations founded classical political economy with theories of division of labour, free markets, and capital accumulation, representing both the Enlightenment heritage (A.1.b) and the development of economic theories (D.1).

Philosophy as the parent discipline of the social sciences, with branches including epistemology, ethics, and political philosophy, provides the intellectual origins from which the social sciences emerged (A.1).

Trade as the exchange of goods and services, covering specialization, comparative advantage, and free trade versus protectionism, falls within the scope and methods of economics including microeconomics and macroeconomics (D.2).

Finance as the discipline of monetary resources, capital allocation, and risk management, distinguished from but connected to economics, aligns with cognate disciplines of economics including mathematical economics and accounting (D.3).
